Calling builds pot OOP with reverse implied odds if he hits (flush/straight); no strong draws of my own. Raising risks dominated calls/shoves. Check-fold exploits his aggression, preserves deep stack for better spots in 2-handed play. Prioritizes discipline over marginal defense.

Heads-up on the button/SB with a big stack advantage (35k vs 4.7k). J2o is weak but within the wide stealing range from SB in heads-up play. Min-raising the blinds has great equity realization as Flash is short and may fold or shove, which I can fold to profitably. Checking OOP with this hand is poor postflop, folding loses dead money unnecessarily. Min-raise to steal.

I am in the Big Blind with Qd4c. Elon, the chip leader, has min-raised to 200 from the Small Blind. I need to call 100 more to see the flop, giving me 4:1 pot odds. While Q4o is a weak hand, it is sufficient to defend against a min-raise in a heads-up situation, especially given the odds and the need to defend my blinds occasionally to prevent being blinded out by the massive chip leader. I will call and see a flop, looking to hit a Queen or a favorable board.

On a board of Ad-Tc-Kh, I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a Jack), but this flop heavily favors the pre-flop raiser's range. As the Big Blind acting first post-flop, I will check to see how Elon responds. My hand has very little showdown value right now and is easily dominated if Elon has any part of this high-card heavy board.

Preflop min-raise with J2o from SB/button was a steal attempt exploiting short stack dynamics and position. Flop A-T-K rainbow hits few strong hands but blocks top of Flash's calling range (no A, T, K specifically). Flash checks OOP as expected. With position, massive stack advantage (35k vs 4.6k), and board favoring continuation range, a standard half-pot cbet applies max pressure. Flash likely folds weak pairs/draws/misses; if called/shoved, easy fold air. No showdown value, pure semi-bluff/value for fold equity. Betting builds pot when ahead or takes it down.

On a board of Ad-Tc-Kh, I have a gutshot straight draw to the nuts. However, this board is extremely favorable for Elon's pre-flop raising range, which likely includes many Aces, Kings, and Tens. My pot odds are 3:1 (200 to call a 600 pot), but the probability of hitting my gutshot on the turn is only about 8.5%. Given my relatively short stack compared to the chip leader and the high likelihood that I will face another bet on the turn, chasing a non-nut draw (since any Jack makes the straight but the board is very coordinated) out of position is not profitable. I will fold to preserve my remaining chips.

Holding 9s 8h on the button (Small Blind), I have a respectable hand for heads-up play. With a stack of 45.5 big blinds, I want to maintain an aggressive posture and use my positional advantage. A standard opening raise to 300 (3x) is appropriate here to test Elon's big blind defense and build a pot in position if called.

Heads-up in BB vs Button/SB raise to 300 (pot 400, call 200). KQo is a premium offsuit broadway hand with good equity against a wide button opening range, which Flash employs frequently based on history (raises button often, light aggression). 3-betting provides fold equity, takes initiative, and sets up postflop advantage despite being OOP. Standard 3bet size ~3x opponent's raise. Large stack disparity (35k vs 4.3k) adds pressure on shorter stack. Calling is viable but 3bet maximizes EV here.

Elon has been demonstrating significant aggression with frequent 3-bets and large post-flop pressure. I opened with 98o, which is a reasonable hand for heads-up play, but facing a 3-bet to 900, calling would commit over 20% of my remaining stack to a pot where I'll likely need to hit the board hard to continue. Given Elon's large stack and willingness to put me to the test, I will fold this hand and preserve my 43 BB stack for a better opportunity or a stronger hand to combat his bullying tactics.

Heads-up on the button/SB with Ah2s, a playable Ace-high hand with position advantage preflop and good blocker to strong Ax hands. Flash has 4200 (42BB), I cover with 35k+. Standard play is to raise for initiative and steal blinds frequently in heads-up. Limp is suboptimal as it invites trouble without initiative. Raising to 3x BB (300 total) is a good size to build pot or take it down preflop against a likely wide BB defending range based on history of calling small raises.

I am in the Big Blind holding Ace-Six offsuit. The opponent on the button raised to 300, which has been a frequent opening size for them. Given that I need to call 200 more into a pot of 400, I am getting 3:1 pot odds. Ace-Six is well ahead of a typical button stealing range in a heads-up scenario. While my stack is significantly smaller than my opponent's, folding here would be too exploitable. Calling allows me to see a flop and utilize my hand's high-card value without overcommitting my remaining chips pre-flop.

On a flop of 7c-8d-5h, I have an open-ended straight draw (4 or 9 will complete it) plus one overcard (Ace). This board texture is generally better for the Big Blind's range than the pre-flop raiser's range. However, standard play is to check to the aggressor. I will check to see how Elon reacts. If he bets, I have sufficient equity with the straight draw to call or potentially check-raise, but for now, checking is the most balanced approach.

Preflop c-bet continuation is standard after raising button vs BB defend and getting a check. Ah2s has ace blocker to top pair Ax hands, decent showdown value as ace-high on this low board (5h-7c-8d rainbow-ish), and backdoor spade flush draw. Board connected but favors wide c-betting range OOP check. Flash has shown tendency to fold to postflop aggression frequently in history (e.g., many folds to c-bets). Half-pot size (300 into 600) builds pot, denies equity to draws, and applies pressure with 40BB effective stack. Checking loses initiative on scary turn potential. Extraction max long-term.

On a flop of 7c-8d-5h, I have an open-ended straight draw with my Ace-Six. Elon's bet of 300 into a 600-chip pot gives me 3:1 pot odds, which is a fantastic price to call given that I have about a 32% chance of making my straight by the river. My hand also has an overcard (the Ace), which could potentially be the best hand if I pair it, although I am primarily playing for the straight. Calling is the most profitable and standard play here to realize my equity without over-inflating the pot while out of position.

On the turn, the board is 7c-8d-5h-2c. I still hold an open-ended straight draw with my Ace-Six. The deuce of clubs is a brick that doesn't change the board texture significantly. As the out-of-position player who called on the flop, the standard play is to check to the aggressor. This allows me to see how Elon evaluates the turn card. If he bets, I will re-evaluate based on the sizing, but I generally want to see a river card as cheaply as possible to try and realize my equity.

Heads-up on button vs BB with Ah2s. Turn 2c gives me bottom pair with strong Ace kicker for showdown value and blocks some straight draws. Board is draw-heavy (club flush draw completed, open-enders like 4-6/6-9/9-T), so betting denies equity to Flash's likely flop calling range (draws, weak pairs, floats). Preflop raise + flop c-bet continuation shows strength; Flash checked turn after calling flop, consistent with history of folding to repeated aggression (e.g., Hands 50,54,60,64,66,70,80,90,94). Effective stacks 37BB for Flash (3700 total, 500 invested), pot 1200. 2/3-pot bet (800) builds pot for value from worse (e.g., 6x, weak 7/5/8), pressures folds from draws/misses, maintains initiative in position. Checking risks giving free card to draws or check-raise bluffs; thin value bet maximizes EV long-term vs folding range.

On a board of 7c-8d-5h-2c, I am holding Ace-Six, giving me an open-ended straight draw (any 4 or 9 completes it, totaling 8 outs). Elon has fired a second barrel of 800 into a 1,200 pot. To call, I need to commit 800 to win a total pot that will become 2,800. This gives me pot odds of 2.5:1 (or approximately 28.5%). My mathematical probability of hitting the straight on the river is about 17.4%. While I don't have the direct pot odds to call, I have significant implied odds because my stack is relatively short (3,100 total at the start of the turn) compared to the pot size. If I hit my straight, I can likely get the rest of my chips in against an aggressive opponent who has shown a willingness to bet multiple streets. Folding an open-ended straight draw heads-up at this stage would be too conservative, and raising doesn't offer enough fold equity against a 2/3 pot bet. I will call to try and realize my equity.

The river 6d gives me a pair of sixes, but the board is now extremely coordinated with 5-6-7-8 showing. Any 4 or 9 in Elon's range (like A9, T9, 99, A4, or 44) has completed a straight. Since I was chasing the straight myself and only ended up with a marginal pair, there is no value in betting. I will check and hope to reach a cheap showdown, though I will likely have to fold to a large bet given how well this board connects with many hands Elon would semi-bluff on the flop and turn.

The river 6d is a very difficult card. While it improves my hand to a pair of sixes, the board is now 5-6-7-8, meaning any 9 or 4 completes a straight. Throughout the hand, Elon has shown significant strength with a pre-flop raise, a flop bet, a turn bet, and now an all-in shove on the river. My pair of sixes is essentially a bluff-catcher, and it's near the bottom of my range considering I was calling specifically to hit the straight with a 9 or a 4. Given Elon's aggression and the high probability that his range contains many 9s, 4s, or higher pairs (77, 88, or even overpairs like 99-AA), I cannot justify calling for my entire tournament life with fourth pair on such a coordinated board. I will fold and preserve my remaining 2,900 chips to look for a better spot.

Heads-up preflop from SB/Button with K9o, a strong hand in this spot (top ~20-25% heads-up). Opponent in BB is short-stacked at ~27.5BB (2750 chips). Standard play is to raise for value, fold equity, and to deny opponent easy equity realization. Limping is suboptimal as it invites shoves or multiway pots we don't want. Raising to 300 (3x the BB) is a good size: builds pot modestly while having fold equity vs their defense range. If they shove, K9o has solid equity (~40-45%) vs a typical BB shoving range (~35-50% of hands). My deep stack (371x BB) allows playing postflop if needed. This maximizes EV over tournament.

Heads-up on the flop with Kh9s on 5s7sJh board. I have the nut flush draw (9s), King-high overcard, and backdoor straight potential. In a heads-up match, middle pair (sevens) with an Ace kicker is a very strong hand to defend against a continuation bet, especially against an aggressive opponent like Elon who has a wide range. Heads-up on turn with Kh9s on 5s7sJh2d board. I have the nut flush draw (9s for nuts, ~19% equity to river flush, higher with live K), K-high overcard, backdoor potential. Facing a small c-bet of 300 into 900 from a short-stacked button opponent with only 850 behind (~8.5BB effective now). Turn brings the Kd, pairing my Kh for top pair top kicker (KQ), plus a strong nut flush draw (Qd with three board diamonds: 8d,4d,Kd). Absolute monster hand (~75-85% equity vs opponent's wide button opening + flop c-bet range, which includes missed overcards, weak pairs like 77-99/JT, flush/straight draws like AdXd/35 suited, backdoors). OOP first to act vs ultra-short stack (850 into 1200, <1BB effective play money wise). Consistent with preflop/flop pot control OOP to exploit shorty: checking induces maximum bluffs/value shoves from worse (Ax/Kx bluffs, sets unlikely/doomed to river jam anyway), which we hero-call for massive +EV (~70%+ equity, great pot odds 850 into 2050). Betting risks: he folds too much worse (lowering EV), check-raises rare monsters (AK rare, blocked by our KQ), commits us awkwardly. Checking realizes full equity, pot controls OOP, sets up river jam if checked through/free showdown value denial. Flash history: aggressive barreling when short (e.g., Hands 1,43,51+ raises bets turn/river light); exploits this perfectly. Maximizes stack growth in HU deep vs short.

The King of Diamonds is a scare card as it is an overcard to my pair of eights and completes potential diamond flushes. River 3c is a complete blank: no flush completes (diamond draw missed), minimal straight help (possible 53 gutshot but niche in button range), doesn't pair low cards meaningfully. Betting risks folding out worse (Ax bluffs fold 70%+ time, lowering EV), small bets get called only by 2p/set rarely; commits awkwardly if shoved.
